title: News from Science Weekly Headlines
html_body:
Brought to you by Eppendorf & Science Prize for Neurobiology

Now accepting entries for the US$ 25,000
Eppendorf & Science Prize for Neurobiology
Application Deadline June 15, 2026
www.eppendorf.com/prize
This week’s headlines
Help News from Science deliver the deep, trustworthy reporting that this challenging time requires.
Support nonprofit science journalism—make a tax-deductible gift.
Latest News
Newton’s law of gravity passes its biggest test ever
Galaxy clusters obey the inverse square law across hundreds of millions of light-years
By ADRIAN CHO  |  1 MAY
Read more
ScienceInsider
Pushed by Trump policies, top U.S. battery scientist is moving to Singapore
Shirley Meng explains why her passion for decarbonizing the global economy required her to relocate
By JEFFREY MERVIS  |  1 MAY
Read more
Latest News
Deep-Earth map reveals a lost U.S. continent
Sensor array traces how rocks conduct electricity, exposing ancient continental fragments, mineral targets, and grid hazards
By PAUL VOOSEN  |  1 MAY
Read more
Latest News
Tiny probes make sense of abnormal bursts in the epileptic brain
“Spikes” hijack neurons involved in cognition—and can be predicted up to 1 second in advance
By JENNIE ERIN SMITH  |  30 APR
Read more
Latest News
AI is starting to beat doctors at making correct diagnoses
Large language model excels at clinical decisions, even in fast pace of a simulated ER
By PERRI THALER  |  30 APR
Read more
Latest News
AI helps create bacterium that’s partially missing a universal amino acid
Advance could suggest new ways to synthesize proteins with bespoke functions in medicine and biotechnology
By CATHERINE OFFORD  |  30 APR
Read more
News Feature
Deepfakes are everywhere. The godfather of digital forensics is fighting back
Hany Farid, who's spent his career building tools to detect fake images, is facing his biggest challenge yet: AI
30 APR
Read more
ScienceInsider
Measles explodes in Bangladesh after vaccination breakdown, killing hundreds of children
Changes to government’s vaccine purchase system after 2024 revolution led to nationwide shortages, gaps in immunity
By ANIK RAHMAN  |  30 APR
Read more
ScienceInsider
After outcry, European funder reverses abrupt rule change restricting resubmissions
Decision from the European Research Council comes after more than 1000 scientists signed open letter decrying plans
By HOLLY ELSE  |  30 APR
Read more
Latest News
As foot-and-mouth disease explodes in South Africa, experts warn of threats in other countries
A viral strain originating in wild buffalo in Kruger National Park is spreading rapidly in cattle
By ELIZABETH PENNISI  |  29 APR
Read more
ScienceInsider
Guns and bulletproof vests: How federal agents arrested Fauci aide
David Morens, 78, was stripped and handcuffed for email violations
By JON COHEN  |  29 APR
Read more
News Feature
To misinformation researchers, AI is a scourge—and a powerful new tool
Surge in generative AI is upending a field that was just finding its feet
29 APR
Read more
Latest News
A novel gene-therapy approach to ‘functionally cure’ HIV succeeds in some monkeys
Six animals suppressed AIDS virus after one shot of treatment that makes cell-receptor antibody
By JON COHEN  |  29 APR
Read more
ScienceInsider
House spending panel bumps up Trump’s request for NSF and NASA
Draft bill is an early sign that Congress again thinks Trump has gone too far in cutting research
By JEFFREY MERVIS  |  29 APR
Read more
Latest News
Enigmatic muscle may help explain penguins’ signature waddle
Find also reveals how the birds keep their bodies streamlined when underwater
By TAYLOR MITCHELL BROWN  |  29 APR
Read more
Latest News
Battle over DNA within fertilized eggs may explain why some IVF procedures fail
Temporarily keeping parents’ genetic contributions separate promotes normal development, mouse study suggests
By MITCH LESLIE  |  29 APR
Read more
Latest News
‘Modern European family’ predates fall of Rome, DNA reveals
Painstaking pedigrees hint at origins and social norms of people living near Rome’s northern frontier
By ANDREW CURRY  |  29 APR
Read more
Latest News
Scorpions reinforce their most lethal weapons with metal
Arachnids infuse zinc into their claws or stingers—whichever they depend on most while hunting
By OLIVIA MAULE  |  28 APR
Read more
ScienceInsider
Fauci aide indicted over federal records violations related to COVID-19
David Morens could face prison time for allegedly concealing back-channel efforts to help nonprofit accused of starting pandemic
By JON COHEN  |  28 APR
Read more
ScienceInsider
Famed Brazilian dinosaur fossil finally heads home
Germany and Brazil reach agreement over controversial spinosaurid fossil, heralding new collaboration between the two nations
By TAYLOR MITCHELL BROWN, RODRIGO PÉREZ ORTEGA  |  28 APR
Read more
Latest News
Bizarre Hawking radiation may smooth the jagged hearts of black holes
Hawking’s signature prediction may prevent vexing singularities from forming
By ADRIAN CHO  |  27 APR
Read more
Latest News
How an HIV/AIDS tragedy spurred human evolution
Before antiretroviral drugs reached South Africa, high death toll shaped immune system genes
By JON COHEN  |  27 APR
Read more
ScienceInsider
Trump fires every member of the U.S. National Science Foundation’s governing body
Dismissal of the National Science Board is widely seen as latest move to erase NSF’s independence
By JEFFREY MERVIS  |  25 APR
Read more
ScienceInsider
Abrupt change to European funder’s rules leaves researchers shut out
Flood of proposals to European Research Council—perhaps unleashed by AI—means unsuccessful applicants must wait longer to reapply
By HOLLY ELSE  |  24 APR
Read more
ScienceInsider
After pulling vaccine study, Bhattacharya criticizes long-running CDC publication
Agency’s temporary director says Morbidity and Mortality Weekly Report needs external reviewers
By JON COHEN  |  24 APR
Read more
Latest News
Can Starship succeed where the space shuttle struggled?
As SpaceX readies its latest megarocket, engineer Stephen Whitmore explains why atmospheric reentry pushes materials to their limits
By PERRI THALER  |  24 APR
Read more
ScienceInsider
UK Biobank faces questions about data security after latest breach
Experts say the lapse highlights that even new measures to control access did not safeguard deidentified patient information
By CATHERINE OFFORD  |  24 APR
Read more
